rawContent:
Tweet by @pauliusztin_ (Paul Iusztin)
Author bio: Senior AI Engineer • Founder @ Decoding AI • Author @ LLM Engineer's Handbook ~ I ship AI products and teach you about the process.
Text: Here's what stood out to me after studying @Neo4j's agent memory system:

Memory is separated into 3 connected layers inside the same graph.

Most AI memory systems today are basically conversation history + vector search.

But @Neo4j approaches it differently...

This is how:

1/ Short-term memory

This is the active conversation state.

A linear chain of messages connected through ordered relationships.

Think:

Current chat history
User turns
Agent responses
Immediate conversational context

This is what keeps the agent coherent during a session and supports the extraction lineage.

2/ Long-term memory

This is the persistent knowledge graph.

It stores:

Entities
Organizations
Locations
Preferences
Facts
Relationships

... as connected graph nodes with embeddings attached.

So instead of retrieving isolated chunks…

The agent traverses connected knowledge.

Example:

Claude Code → Anthropic → San Francisco → Codex → Gemini CLI

So memory becomes relational instead of purely semantic.

3/ Reasoning memory

This was the most interesting part to me.

@Neo4j stores reasoning traces as their own graph structure:

Thoughts
Tool calls
Decision paths
Intermediate reasoning steps

Essentially, a tree per agent run.

This means the system can later analyze:

What reasoning paths succeeded
Which tools were useful
What strategies failed
How the answer was constructed

It feels very similar to reinforcement learning…

Except the optimization happens at the database layer instead of the model weights.

And all 3 memory types are stitched together through typed edges:

:MENTIONS
:INITIATED_BY
:TOUCHED

This is what makes provenance a one-hop query instead of a reconstruction problem.

My biggest takeaway?

The ontology is the real product.

Once memory becomes graph-native, retrieval becomes:

Traversing relationships
Following reasoning paths
Understanding structure over time

This is a very different way to think about AI memory systems.

P.S. I wrote a full deep dive on @Neo4j’s Agent Memory architecture in Decoding AI Magazine if you want the complete breakdown.

Check it out here: https://t.co/RHYv94dvB6
